risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization to the three treatment groups was stratified by participant’s baseline chronic pain grade and recruitment source ... with blocking ... Block sizes were equal to 3 or 6, and were chosen randomly with 2/3 and 1/3 probability, respectively ... The randomization list was prepared using the “sample” function of the S-PLUS® statistical software ..." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process with stratification, randomly chosen block sizes, and computer/software-generated randomization, which is sufficient for Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: Randomization to the three treatment groups was stratified by participant’s baseline chronic pain grade and recruitment source (U.W. Orofacial Pain Clinic new patient, U.W. Orofacial Pain Clinic return patient, and non-U.W. sources), and with blocking to ensure that the groups would be (nearly) balanced across the study period and within each stratum. Block sizes were equal to 3 or 6, and were chosen randomly with 2/3 and 1/3 probability, respectively. In addition, the number of women randomized to the COCT group was increased by 25% to allow for some disqualification at the ARNP visit. The randomization list was prepared using the “sample” function of the S-PLUS® statistical software (Insightful Corporation, Seattle, WA).
_evidence.method_summary: Stratified blocked randomization with randomly chosen block sizes generated using S-PLUS sample function.
context_clues: Block sizes were equal to 3 or 6
sample function of the S-PLUS® statistical software

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Treatment assignments were recorded on cards numbered consecutively within each stratum, and a study assistant not involved in the screening and randomization put the randomization assignments in sealed envelopes sequentially numbered by stratum. Randomization assignments were concealed to all study personnel with study participant contact until envelopes were opened by research staff at the time of randomization." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered sealed-envelope procedure with assignments concealed from study personnel until the point of randomization, which prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Treatment assignments were recorded on cards numbered consecutively within each stratum, and a study assistant not involved in the screening and randomization put the randomization assignments in sealed envelopes sequentially numbered by stratum. Randomization assignments were concealed to all study personnel with study participant contact until envelopes were opened by research staff at the time of randomization.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Potential participants were told that the study involved randomization to COCT or to one of two different versions of SMT. At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes." Comment: Participants were explicitly not told the key distinguishing feature of one intervention, indicating incomplete participant blinding. The interventions were also clearly different in content and delivery (self-management sessions versus continuous oral contraceptive therapy), so participants and delivering personnel could plausibly have known or inferred allocation. Because the main outcomes were largely subjective/self-reported (pain intensity, activity interference, depression, beliefs, perceived helpfulness), knowledge of assignment could plausibly affect outcomes, so this domain is High risk.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Potential participants were told that the study involved randomization to COCT or to one of two different versions of SMT. At no time before or during study participation were participants informed that one and only one version focused on menstrual cycle-related changes.
outcome_categories: perceived effectiveness of pain coping strategies
subjective_outcomes: perceived effectiveness of pain coping strategies

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants completed the study measures at the time of study enrollment (baseline, prior to randomization) and at 6 and 12 months after randomization." and "The Graded Chronic Pain Scale (GCPS) was used to assess pain intensity and interference with usual daily activities." Comment: The outcome assessor was the patient self-reporting the outcomes, including characteristic pain intensity, activity interference, SF-MPQ, BDI, treatment helpfulness, SOPA, self-efficacy, catastrophizing, and perceived effectiveness. Because these are subjective/patient-reported outcomes, and the study does not state that participants were blinded at follow-up (participants were only told they were randomized to treatments, while the actual treatments were obviously different), knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of multiple unblinded subjective outcomes makes the overall domain high risk.

outcomes.label: perceived effectiveness of pain coping strategies

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “In each of the three groups, 86% of participants completed at least one follow-up assessment.” “Among the 191 women who were randomized, 20 were withdrawn from the study (15 of whom were randomized to the COCT group but then withdrawn due to medical contra-indications).” “The primary analyses comparing the three study groups were intent-to-treat analyses, which included all randomized participants for whom follow-up data (either at 6 months or 12 months) were available...” “In addition, we performed multiple imputation analyses ... to assess the effect of missing data due to participants who provided no follow-up data.” Comment: There was substantial post-randomization missing outcome data, with marked imbalance across arms: SMT 51/60 analyzed (9 lost), TSMT 47/57 analyzed (10 lost), and COCT 49/74 analyzed (25 lost), giving >20% overall loss and a >10 percentage-point differential between arms. The COCT arm also had outcome-related exclusions/withdrawals (e.g., never starting due to deciding after randomization not to take medication; side effects; medical contraindications), and the paper reports as-treated analyses in addition to ITT, indicating potential bias from incomplete outcome data.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “both of the in-person self-management treatment sessions were completed by 46 (77%) of the 60 women randomized to SMT”; “The analysis sample (n = 147)” and “n = 51” in Table 1. “no follow-up data; n = 24” for excluded participants; specific reasons for loss not given for SMT.

“both of the in-person self-management treatment sessions were completed by 47 (82%) of the 57 women randomized to TSMT”; “The analysis sample (n = 147)” and “n = 47” in Table 1. “no follow-up data; n = 24” for excluded participants; specific reasons for loss not given for TSMT.

“Among the COCT analysis sample, 51% completed the COCT protocol, 33% never started the medication (most because they decided after randomization that they did not want to take the medication), and 16% started but did not complete the medication protocol (most because of side effects).” “In sum, among 74 women randomized to the COCT arm of the study, only 25 (34%) completed the entire COC protocol.” “The analysis sample (n = 147)” and “n = 49” in Table 1. Also, 15 of the 20 withdrawals were “randomized to the COCT group but then withdrawn due to medical contra-indications.”

_evidence.missing_data_method: ITT; as-treated; multiple imputation; complete-case for available follow-up data
_evidence.notes: Primary ITT analysis included all randomized participants with at least one follow-up; 24 participants had no follow-up data and were excluded from analysis. COCT also had substantial protocol non-completion, and as-treated analyses were reported separately.
